title: Discrete signaling mediates chaotic regularization in recurrent neural networks
authors: Jan Bauer, Christian Keup, Jonathan Kadmon, Moritz Helias
abstract: Cortical circuits operate in a regime of intrinsic chaos, where even tiny changes in input can lead to divergent neural responses. Yet, remarkably, population codes in the brain vary smoothly with sensory stimuli, forming coherent representational manifolds. How can chaotic networks sustain such stable coding? Here, we develop a theoretical framework that links the microscopic chaos of recurrent networks to the macroscopic geometry of neural representations. Combining kernel methods with dynamical mean-field theory, we show that chaotic dynamics induce local roughness (introducing sharp distortions at small scales) while preserving global smoothness across larger stimulus variations. This structural property acts as an intrinsic regularizer, enhancing generalization while maintaining expressivity. Moreover, we show how chaotic networks naturally produce power-law spectral signatures, closely matching experimental observations in cortical recordings. These results explain how chaotic spiking networks can sustain smooth, differentiable population codes and establish a theoretical framework linking network dynamics, computational structure, and recorded neural activity.
summary: This theoretical work develops a framework showing that the inherent chaos in recurrent neural networks does not preclude stable computation. By analyzing the network's kernel structure using advanced mean-field techniques, the authors prove that this chaos acts as a beneficial intrinsic regularizer, enabling smooth, generalizable coding despite microscopic unpredictability.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental conflict between the intrinsic chaotic nature of recurrent neural networks and the necessity for their population codes to exhibit smooth, stable representations for functional computation.
main_result: Chaotic dynamics induce a structural property—local roughness while maintaining global smoothness—that acts as an intrinsic regularizer, allowing chaotic systems to sustain coherent, differentiable coding.
method: The authors combine kernel methods with Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) and the replica method to derive the effective kernel describing the network's representation geometry.
model_or_system: The study analyzes Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) operating in regimes of intrinsic chaos, comparing the theoretical predictions derived from continuous-rate models against those from discrete, spiking (Glauber) dynamics.
key_observables: Representation geometry (smoothness/roughness), power-law spectral signatures, and the two-replica kernel function $k_{ts}(x_1 \cdot x_2)$.
important_parameters: Synaptic strength ($g$), network size ($N$), and the ratio of recurrent to feedforward coupling ($g/g_V$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies heavily on the large-$N$ limit (mean-field theory) and the assumption that the system's statistics can be captured by the two-replica correlation function.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison of classification accuracy and kernel functions between chaotic discrete networks and regular/feedforward models, demonstrating the regularization effect of local chaos.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the theoretical link between chaos and representation geometry, then deriving the mean-field equations for the two-replica kernel using DMFT, and finally contrasting the resulting kernel structure for continuous versus discrete signaling dynamics.
why_interesting: The mathematical machinery developed (DMFT, replica method) for analyzing correlations in complex, non-equilibrium systems like RNNs shares conceptual overlap with techniques used in analyzing disordered quantum systems, such as spin glasses or spin-boson models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Describing computation in RNNs in kernel space. a A recurrent neural network (RNN) receives an input stimulus x at an initial time t0. Figure 2. Chaotic network models can perform reliable computation despite rough neural code. a Four exam- ples from the image dataset CIFAR10. b Accuracy of the binary classification between cars and planes as a function of the number of presented training samples P. Blue: Heaviside- activated (i.e., T = H) recurrent network with Glauber dy- namics as in Eq. (3) with g = 1.1. Gray: Linear classifier. Dashed black: Single-layer Heaviside-activated feedforward network. Readout time for the recurrent network, and regu- larization for the feedforward network were optimized in the networks to give best performance for each number of train- ing patterns. Figure 3. Strong local chaos acts as effective regular- ization in discretely-coupled networks. a Inference for a continuously signaling network Eq. (4), b for discrete sig- naling Eq. (3). Left panels show the kernel functions for each network.
